ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Singaporean economic rice with mixed meat toppings; mapped to broader cooked rice dish concept.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for Singaporean economic rice; A00LC (rice-based composite dish) is the closest defensible analogous code.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for this specific Singaporean mixed rice dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of economic rice with two meat dishes (one fried) typically weighs approximately 350 g, comprising roughly 200 g of steamed white rice and 150 g of mixed meat toppings.

nutrient_source.title: Economic Rice (2 meats, 1 fried) — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Economic Rice (2 meats, 1 fried), CR ID F05100036. All per-100g values are exact matches from this source.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — white rice (cooked, Singaporean/Asian preparation)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 72 is based on the University of Sydney GI Database value for cooked white rice (Asian preparation), which is the dominant carbohydrate component of this dish. The composite dish GI is estimated from the white rice base (GI ~72, high), consistent with the HPB input record. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (72 × 70.07) / 100 ≈ 50.4, using a 350 g serving with 20.02 g carbohydrate per 100 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium at 258 mg/100 g with minimal dietary fibre (0.74 g/100 g) and no vegetable component to provide potassium offset; fried meat contributes saturated fat promoting arterial stiffness.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size, replace the fried meat with a steamed or braised option, and add a vegetable dish to improve potassium and fibre intake, as the sodium content of 258 mg/100 g with negligible potassium offset poses a meaningful cardiovascular risk.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High-GI white rice base (GI 72) with 20.02 g carbohydrate per 100 g and only 0.74 g dietary fibre produces a very high glycaemic load per serving, driving rapid postprandial glucose elevation.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with type 2 diabetes should significantly reduce the rice portion, substitute brown or parboiled rice where possible, and add a vegetable dish to slow glucose absorption, as the high glycaemic load of approximately 50 per standard serving is clinically unsuitable for glycaemic control.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Deep-fried meat component introduces advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) and elevated saturated fat; sodium at 258 mg/100 g and absence of vegetables or omega-3 sources provide no cardioprotective offset.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid the fried meat component, replace it with steamed fish or braised lean protein, reduce the rice portion, and add a vegetable dish, as the combination of saturated fat from frying, elevated sodium, and absence of cardioprotective nutrients makes this dish high-risk for regular consumption.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying introduces AGEs which promote systemic inflammation; no anti-inflammatory nutrients (omega-3, antioxidants, polyphenols, cruciferous vegetables) are present in this dish combination.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should replace the fried meat with steamed fish or braised chicken and add at least one vegetable dish, as the absence of any anti-inflammatory dietary factor combined with AGE-generating deep-frying makes this dish pro-inflammatory.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Economic Rice (2 Meats, 1 Fried) is a ubiquitous Singaporean hawker centre meal consisting of steamed white rice served with two meat-based side dishes, one of which is deep-fried (such as fried chicken cutlet, fried fish, or fried pork) and one of which is braised, stir-fried, or otherwise cooked without deep-frying. Known locally as 'cai fan' (菜饭) or 'economy rice', this dish is a staple of Singapore's food culture, available at virtually every hawker centre and coffee shop (kopitiam) across the island. The rice is typically long-grain jasmine or Thai fragrant rice, steamed to a soft, slightly sticky texture. The fried meat component contributes a crispy exterior and rich savoury flavour, while the second meat dish adds variety in texture and seasoning. Per 100 g, this dish provides 199 kcal, 9.65 g protein, 8.69 g fat, and 20.02 g carbohydrate, with 258 mg sodium and only 0.74 g dietary fibre. The high-GI white rice base (GI 72) combined with low fibre content and the absence of vegetables creates a high glycaemic load per serving (approximately 50), making this dish clinically unsuitable for individuals managing type 2 diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease without significant modification. The fried meat component also introduces advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) from deep-frying, which are pro-inflammatory. Nutritional improvement strategies include reducing the rice portion, substituting brown rice, replacing the fried meat with a steamed or braised option, and adding a vegetable dish to improve fibre, potassium, and antioxidant intake.
common_user_queries: How many calories in cai fan with 2 meats?
Is economic rice suitable for hypertension?
Economic rice with fried chicken calories

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (CR ID F05100036), which is the primary approved source for Singaporean dishes. The source match is exact. GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database for cooked white rice (Asian preparation), which is the dominant carbohydrate component and an approved GI source.
notes: Saturated fat estimated at 2.5 g/100 g based on typical composition of a mixed fried and braised meat dish over white rice; HPB source does not report saturated fat separately for this entry. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1 as these values are not reported in the HPB Foodo database entry for this composite dish and are not available from any other approved source for this specific preparation. Calcium (6.0 mg/100 g) and iron (0.16 mg/100 g) are reported directly from HPB. Halal status is marked false as the dish may contain pork depending on the hawker stall; consumers should verify with the individual vendor. Glycemic load per serving calculated using a 350 g standard plate serving: (72 × 20.02 × 3.5) / 100 = 50.4.
